Subject: Electrical power equipment for heavy guns
Proposed By: Sprague Electric Co., General Electric Co.
Subject Details: Electrical equipments were incorporated into guns for the purpose of increasing the velocity of the projectiles. It incorporated an existing technology of electrical generators that were developed during the period.
Proposer Details:
Sprague Electric Co.: Electric company formed by Frank Sprague, who served in the Navy during the late 19th century. Sprague developed electric motors when working for Edison. 

General Electric Co.: GE later became involved with supplying the military with various equipments, including ships, submarines, weapons-control systems and motors for aircrafts during the Second World War.
Recommendation Reasoning: The military was looking into creating a different type of firearm that utilized electricity coils as opposed to gunpowder for propulsion. Both companies involved with the invention were responsible for producing electric mortars and GE became a prominent partner with the US military.

Subject: Brown wire-wound gun
Subject Details: Gun with specific design that incorporated wrapping layers of steel wire around a central tube for a stronger, reinfornced structure. The gun was revolutionary for creating a construction system for heavy ordnance.
Proposer Details: Brown and Munsellwas part of the group  Birdsboro Steel to manufacture the Brown-wire wound gun. Birdsboro Steel manufactured ordnance equipment such as ammunitions, tanks, and artilleries for the US military. Brown and Munsell later sued the US government on 1918.
Recommendation Reasoning: Brown-wire gun was developed by one of the older manufacturing companies. Despite being considered a revolutionary weaponry with construction system specifically tailored for the production of heavy artillery, it soon became irrelevant after its initial uses. It is interesting to explore why some other guns were favored over the Brown-wire gun later down the line.
